title: Niam-ut-Tawi-zul-Ali
gloss: An Urdu translation, by Muhammad Din, of Nasihat-ut-Talmiz of Imam Ghazali, containing certain admonitions intended for one of his disciples on the benefit of knowledge, good deeds, devotion, contemplation &c.
notes: Al-Ghazali's Ayyuha'l-Walad (Nasihat al-Talamidh) translated into Urdu — the great Sufi-theologian's letter of counsel; translator is a Chief-Court Judge of Bahawalpur (elite-official authorship). Printed reg 249 also appears at p28 s12 (Ramuz-i-Haqiqat) — likely one is a misread; flagged.

flags.issue: printed reg 249 also recorded at p28 s12 (Ramuz-i-Haqiqat); possible misread/collision

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Jam-i-Jahan Numa
gloss: A controversial book in which the writer tries to show that the first three caliphs of the prophet Muhammad were usurpers and in no way fit for the caliphate, and that the only rightful claimant was Ali, the prophet's son-in-law.
notes: Another Shia imamate polemic denouncing the first three caliphs as usurpers — a whole cluster of Shia controversial works (s16-19) from the Yusufi/Newal Kishore presses; the Shia-Sunni succession dispute saturates this section. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Mashal-i-Hidayat
gloss: A controversial book in which the writer tries to answer objections raised to the direct succession of Ali to the caliphate after the prophet Muhammad.
notes: Author dash-continuation of s16 (Sajjad Hussain). Defends Ali's direct succession — the famous Newal Kishore Press (Lucknow's great publisher, here with a Lahore imprint) in the Shia-polemic trade. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Aftab-i-Khilafat
gloss: A controversial book in which the writer tries to prove that Ali was the only rightful successor of the prophet Muhammad.
notes: Author dash-continuation of s17 (Sajjad Hussain). 'Sun of the Caliphate' — third of his Shia-succession trilogy this quarter. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sajjad Hussain, M.
title: Sharh-i-Kauz-i-Maktum
gloss: A controversial book criticising the Sunni belief that Ali, the fourth Caliph, of his own free will, married his daughter, named Umm-i-Kalsum, to Umar, the second Caliph. The author has presented the case in a legal form in which the Sunnis are complainants and the Shias defendants; issues are framed; and after listening to the evidence produced by the parties and their counsels, judgment is delivered on the merits of the case, which is in favour of the Shias.
notes: A remarkable Shia polemic cast as a MOCK COURT TRIAL (Sunnis as plaintiffs, Shias as defendants, framed issues, evidence, verdict for the Shias) over the Umm Kulthum-Umar marriage — the colonial legal-procedural form appropriated as a rhetorical device for sectarian argument; a vivid instance of the courtroom paradigm colonizing religious disputation (directly on-theme: the imperial juridical form reifying/reshaping indigenous debate). Author Sajjad Hussain's fourth Shia work this quarter. Dual date 1327 Hijri.

author: Sanaullah, Maulvi
title: Ittiba-i-Salaf
gloss: A controversial book in which the writer has tried to show that the Muhammadans are not bound to follow the interpretations of the Sahabis or the prophet's followers in his life time, as they are liable to err in the Hadises or traditions.
notes: An Ahl-i-Hadith (salafi) tract by the famous Maulvi Sanaullah Amritsari, arguing Muslims need not follow the Companions' interpretations — the Ahl-i-Hadith vs taqlid (Hanafi/traditionalist) controversy; from the movement's own Amritsar press. Yet another distinct sectarian front (Ahl-i-Hadith reformism) in this dense polemical section.
